* [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland_dialect_group "Greater Poland dialect group") * [Goral](/wiki/Goral_ethnolect "Goral ethnolect") * [Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland_dialect_group "Lesser Poland dialect group") * [Masovian](/wiki/Masovian_dialect_group "Masovian dialect group") * [New mixed dialects](/wiki/New_mixed_dialects "New mixed dialects") * [Northern Borderlands](/wiki/Northern_Borderlands_dialect "Northern Borderlands dialect") * [Silesian](/wiki/Silesian_language "Silesian language")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[a]](#cite_note-silesian-6) * [Southern Borderlands](/wiki/Southern_Borderlands_dialect "Southern Borderlands dialect")

For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

([endonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym"): *język polski*, [[ˈjɛ̃zɨk ˈpɔlskʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-j%C4%99zyk_polski.ogg "File:Pl-język polski.ogg"), *polszczyzna* [[pɔlˈʂt͡ʂɨzna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-polszczyzna.ogg "File:Pl-polszczyzna.ogg") or simply *polski*, [[ˈpɔlskʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-polski.ogg "File:Pl-polski.ogg")) is a [West Slavic language](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") of the [Lechitic subgroup](/wiki/Lechitic_languages "Lechitic languages"), within the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), and is written in the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script").[[13]](#cite_note-14) It is primarily spoken in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and serves as the official language of the country, as well as the language of the [Polish diaspora](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora") around the world. In 2024, there were over 39.7 million Polish native speakers.[[14]](#cite_note-ethnologue-15) It ranks as the sixth-most-spoken among [languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union").[[15]](#cite_note-16) Polish is subdivided into regional [dialects](/wiki/Dialects_of_Polish "Dialects of Polish"). It maintains strict [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction") pronouns, [honorifics](/wiki/Honorifics_(linguistics) "Honorifics (linguistics)"), and various forms of formalities when addressing individuals.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

**Polish** ([endonym](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym"): *język polski*, [[ˈjɛ̃zɨk ˈpɔlskʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-j%C4%99zyk_polski.ogg "File:Pl-język polski.ogg"), *polszczyzna* [[pɔlˈʂt͡ʂɨzna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-polszczyzna.ogg "File:Pl-polszczyzna.ogg") or simply *polski*, [[ˈpɔlskʲi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-polski.ogg "File:Pl-polski.ogg")) is a [West Slavic language](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages") of the [Lechitic subgroup](/wiki/Lechitic_languages "Lechitic languages"), within the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"), and is written in the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script").[[13]](#cite_note-14) It is primarily spoken in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and serves as the official language of the country, as well as the language of the [Polish diaspora](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora") around the world. In 2024, there were over 39.7 million Polish native speakers.[[14]](#cite_note-ethnologue-15) It ranks as the sixth-most-spoken among [languages of the European Union](/wiki/Languages_of_the_European_Union "Languages of the European Union").[[15]](#cite_note-16) Polish is subdivided into regional [dialects](/wiki/Dialects_of_Polish "Dialects of Polish"). It maintains strict [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction") pronouns, [honorifics](/wiki/Honorifics_(linguistics) "Honorifics (linguistics)"), and various forms of formalities when addressing individuals.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The traditional 32-letter [Polish alphabet](/wiki/Polish_alphabet "Polish alphabet") has nine additions (*ą*, *ć*, *ę*, *ł*, *ń*, *ó*, *ś*, *ź*, *ż*) to the letters of the basic 26-letter [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"), while removing three (x, q, v). Those three letters are at times included in an extended 35-letter alphabet.[[17]](#cite_note-sjp.pwn.pl-18) The traditional set comprises 23 [consonants](/wiki/Consonants "Consonants") and nine written [vowels](/wiki/Vowels "Vowels"), including two [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowels "Nasal vowels") (*ę*, *ą*) denoted by a reversed [diacritic](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") hook called an *[ogonek](/wiki/Ogonek "Ogonek")*.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Polish is a [synthetic and fusional](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language") language which has seven [grammatical cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case").[[19]](#cite_note-20) It has fixed [penultimate stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") and an abundance of [palatal consonants](/wiki/Palatal_consonants "Palatal consonants").[[20]](#cite_note-21) Contemporary Polish developed in the 1700s as the successor to the medieval [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish "Old Polish") (10th–16th centuries) and [Middle Polish](/wiki/Middle_Polish "Middle Polish") (16th–18th centuries).[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Among the major languages, it is most closely related to [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language")[[22]](#cite_note-23) and [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language")[[23]](#cite_note-Swan-2002-24) but differs in terms of pronunciation and general grammar. In addition, Polish was profoundly influenced by [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and other [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") like [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") as well as [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") (most notably [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")), which contributed a large number of loanwords and similar grammatical structures.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) Extensive usage of [nonstandard dialects](/wiki/Nonstandard_dialect "Nonstandard dialect") has also shaped the [standard language](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"); many [colloquialisms](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") and expressions were directly borrowed from German or [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") and subsequently adopted into the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") of Polish in everyday use.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Historically, Polish was a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*,[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) important both diplomatically and academically in [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and part of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"). In addition to being the official language of Poland, Polish is also spoken as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") in eastern [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), northern [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), western parts of [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") as well as in southeast [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"). Because of the emigration from Poland during different time periods, most notably after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), millions of Polish speakers can also be found in countries such as [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Polish](/wiki/History_of_Polish "History of Polish")

See also: [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish "Old Polish") and [Middle Polish](/wiki/Middle_Polish "Middle Polish")

Polish began to emerge as a distinct language around the 10th century, the process largely triggered by the establishment and development of the Polish state. At the time, it was a collection of dialect groups with some mutual features, but much regional variation was present.[[31]](#cite_note-32) [Mieszko I](/wiki/Mieszko_I_of_Poland "Mieszko I of Poland"), ruler of the Polans tribe from the [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland") region, united a few culturally and linguistically related tribes from the basins of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula") and [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") before eventually accepting baptism in 966. With the arrival of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), Poland also adopted the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"), which made it possible to write down Polish, which until then had existed only as a [spoken language](/wiki/Spoken_language "Spoken language").[[32]](#cite_note-33) The closest relatives of Polish are the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe_Slavs "Elbe Slavs") and [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Pomeranians_(tribe) "Pomeranians (tribe)") Lechitic dialects ([Polabian](/wiki/Polabian_language "Polabian language") and [Pomeranian](/wiki/Pomeranian_language "Pomeranian language") varieties). All of them, except [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), are extinct.[[33]](#cite_note-Kolaczyk-34) The precursor to modern Polish is the [Old Polish language](/wiki/Old_Polish_language "Old Polish language"). Ultimately, Polish descends from the unattested [Proto-Slavic](/wiki/Proto-Slavic "Proto-Slavic") language.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG/250px-Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG)](/wiki/File:Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w.PNG)

The [Book of Henryków](/wiki/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w "Book of Henryków") is the earliest document to include a sentence written entirely in what can be interpreted as [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish_language "Old Polish language") – *Day, ut ia pobrusa, a ti poziwai*, meaning "let me grind, and you have a rest" highlighted in red.

The [Book of Henryków](/wiki/Book_of_Henryk%C3%B3w "Book of Henryków") (Polish: *Księga henrykowska*, [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Liber fundationis claustri Sanctae Mariae Virginis in Heinrichau*), contains the earliest known sentence written in the Polish language: *Day, ut ia pobrusa, a ti poziwai* (in modern orthography: *Daj, uć ja pobrusza, a ti pocziwaj*; the corresponding sentence in modern Polish: *Daj, niech ja pomielę, a ty odpoczywaj* or *Pozwól, że ja będę mełł, a ty odpocznij*; and in English: *Come, let me grind, and you take a rest*), written around 1280. The book is exhibited in the Archdiocesal Museum in Wrocław, and as of 2015 has been added to [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s "[Memory of the World](/wiki/Memory_of_the_World_Programme "Memory of the World Programme")" list.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The medieval recorder of this phrase, the Cistercian monk Peter of the Henryków monastery, noted that "Hoc est in polonico" ("This is in Polish").[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

The earliest treatise on Polish orthography was written by [Jakub Parkosz](/w/index.php?title=Jakub_Parkosz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jakub Parkosz (page does not exist)") [[pl](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jakub_Parkoszowic "pl:Jakub Parkoszowic")] around 1470.[[38]](#cite_note-39) The first [printed](/wiki/History_of_printing_in_Poland "History of printing in Poland") book in Polish appeared in either 1508[[39]](#cite_note-40) or 1513,[[40]](#cite_note-Comrie-41) while the [oldest Polish newspaper](/wiki/Merkuriusz_Polski_Ordynaryjny "Merkuriusz Polski Ordynaryjny") was established in 1661.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Starting in the 1520s, large numbers of books in the Polish language were published, contributing to increased homogeneity of grammar and orthography.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The writing system achieved its overall form in the 16th century,[[33]](#cite_note-Kolaczyk-34)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamusella2009111-44) which is also regarded as the "[Golden Age](/wiki/Polish_Golden_Age "Polish Golden Age") of Polish literature".[[40]](#cite_note-Comrie-41) The [orthography was modified](/wiki/History_of_Polish_orthography "History of Polish orthography") in the 19th century and in 1936.[[33]](#cite_note-Kolaczyk-34)

[Tomasz Kamusella](/wiki/Tomasz_Kamusella "Tomasz Kamusella") notes that "Polish is the oldest, non-ecclesiastical, written Slavic language with a continuous tradition of literacy and official use, which has lasted unbroken from the 16th century to this day."[[44]](#cite_note-45) Polish evolved into the main [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") of the nobles in [Poland–Lithuania](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_union "Polish–Lithuanian union") in the 15th century.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamusella2009111-44) The history of Polish as a language of state governance begins in the 16th century in the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland"). Over the later centuries, Polish served as the official language in the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland"), the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria"), and as the administrative language in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire")'s [Western Krai](/wiki/Western_Krai "Western Krai"). The growth of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")'s influence gave Polish the status of *lingua franca* in [Central and Eastern Europe](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_Europe "Central and Eastern Europe").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamusella2009137-46)

The process of standardization began in the 14th century[[46]](#cite_note-47) and solidified in the 16th century during the Middle Polish era.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Standard Polish was based on various dialectal features, with the [Greater Poland dialect group](/wiki/Greater_Poland_dialect_group "Greater Poland dialect group") serving as the base.[[48]](#cite_note-49) After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Standard Polish became the most widely spoken variant of Polish across the country, and most dialects stopped being the form of Polish spoken in villages.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

See also: [Geographical distribution of Polish speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Polish_speakers "Geographical distribution of Polish speakers")

Poland is one of the most linguistically [homogeneous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/homogeneous "wiktionary:homogeneous") European countries; nearly 97% of Poland's citizens declare Polish as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language"). Elsewhere, [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") constitute large minorities in areas which were once administered or occupied by Poland, notably in neighboring [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). Polish is the most widely used minority language in Lithuania's [Vilnius County](/wiki/Vilnius_County "Vilnius County"), by 26% of the population, according to the 2001 census results, as [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius") was part of Poland from 1922 until 1939. Polish is found elsewhere in southeastern Lithuania. In Ukraine, it is most common in the western parts of [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv_Oblast "Lviv Oblast") and [Volyn Oblasts](/wiki/Volyn_Oblast "Volyn Oblast"), while in [West Belarus](/wiki/West_Belarus "West Belarus") it is used by the significant Polish minority, especially in the [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_Belarus "Brest, Belarus") and [Grodno](/wiki/Grodno "Grodno") regions and in areas along the Lithuanian border. There are significant numbers of Polish speakers among Polish emigrants and their descendants in many other countries.

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") number more than 11 million but most of them cannot speak Polish fluently. According to the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), 667,414 Americans of age five years and over reported Polish as the language spoken at home, which is about 1.4% of people who speak languages other than [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), 0.25% of the US population, and 6% of the Polish-American population. The largest concentrations of Polish speakers reported in the census (over 50%) were found in three states: [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") (185,749), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (111,740), and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") (74,663).[[50]](#cite_note-51) Enough people in these areas speak Polish that [PNC Financial Services](/wiki/PNC_Financial_Services "PNC Financial Services") (which has a large number of branches in all of these areas) offers services available in Polish at all of their [cash machines](/wiki/Cash_machine "Cash machine") in addition to [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[51]](#cite_note-52)

According to the 2011 census there are now over 500,000 people in [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") who consider Polish to be their "main" language. In [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), there is a significant [Polish Canadian population](/wiki/Polish_Canadians "Polish Canadians"): There are 242,885 speakers of Polish according to the 2006 census; in particular, there are concentrations of Polish speakers in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") (91,810 speakers) and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal").[[52]](#cite_note-census-53)

The geographical distribution of the Polish language was greatly affected by the [territorial changes of Poland immediately after World War II](/wiki/Territorial_changes_of_Poland_immediately_after_World_War_II "Territorial changes of Poland immediately after World War II") and [Polish population transfers (1944–46)](/wiki/Polish_population_transfers_(1944%E2%80%9346) "Polish population transfers (1944–46)"). Poles settled in the "[Recovered Territories](/wiki/Recovered_Territories "Recovered Territories")" in the west and north, which had previously been mostly [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")-speaking. Some Poles remained in the previously Polish-ruled territories in the east that were annexed by the [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), resulting in the present-day Polish-speaking communities in Lithuania, Belarus, and Ukraine, although many Poles were expelled from those areas to areas within Poland's new borders. To the east of Poland, the most significant Polish minority lives in a long strip along either side of the [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania")-[Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus") border. Meanwhile, the [flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–50)](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%9350) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–50)"), as well as the [expulsion of Ukrainians](/wiki/Population_exchange_between_Poland_and_Soviet_Ukraine "Population exchange between Poland and Soviet Ukraine") and [Operation Vistula](/wiki/Operation_Vistula "Operation Vistula"), the 1947 migration of Ukrainian minorities in the Recovered Territories in the west of the country, contributed to the country's linguistic homogeneity.

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Main article: [Dialects of Polish](/wiki/Dialects_of_Polish "Dialects of Polish")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Modlitwy_drukowane_po_polsku_w_r._1475.JPG/250px-Modlitwy_drukowane_po_polsku_w_r._1475.JPG)](/wiki/File:Modlitwy_drukowane_po_polsku_w_r._1475.JPG)

The oldest printed text in Polish – *Statuta synodalia Episcoporum Wratislaviensis* printed in 1475 in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław") by Kasper Elyan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Polish-alphabet.png/250px-Polish-alphabet.png)](/wiki/File:Polish-alphabet.png)

The Polish alphabet contains 32 letters. Q, V and X are not used in the Polish language.

The inhabitants of different regions of Poland still[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit) speak Polish somewhat differently, although the differences between modern-day [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") varieties and standard Polish (*język ogólnopolski*) appear relatively slight. Most of the middle aged and young speak vernaculars close to standard Polish, while the traditional dialects are preserved among older people in rural areas.[[53]](#cite_note-wtad-54) First-language speakers of Polish have no trouble understanding each other, and non-native speakers may have difficulty recognizing the regional and [social](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") differences. The modern [standard dialect](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"), often termed as "correct Polish",[[53]](#cite_note-wtad-54) is spoken or at least understood throughout the entire country.[[23]](#cite_note-Swan-2002-24)

Polish has traditionally been described as consisting of three to five main regional dialects:

* [Greater Polish](/wiki/Greater_Polish_dialect "Greater Polish dialect"), spoken in the west
* [Lesser Polish](/wiki/Lesser_Polish_dialect "Lesser Polish dialect"), spoken in the south and southeast
* [Masovian](/wiki/Masovian_dialect "Masovian dialect"), spoken throughout the central and eastern parts of the country

[Silesian](/wiki/Silesian_language "Silesian language") and [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language"), spoken in [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") and [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") respectively, are thought of as either [Polish dialects](/wiki/Dialects_of_Polish "Dialects of Polish") or [distinct languages](/wiki/Ausbau "Ausbau"), depending on the criteria used.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

Kashubian contains a number of features not found elsewhere in Poland, e.g. nine distinct oral vowels (vs. the six of standard Polish) and (in the northern dialects) phonemic word stress, an archaic feature preserved from [Common Slavic](/wiki/Common_Slavic "Common Slavic") times and not found anywhere else among the [West Slavic languages](/wiki/West_Slavic_languages "West Slavic languages"). However, it was described by some linguists as lacking most of the linguistic and social determinants of language-hood.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Many linguistic sources categorize Silesian as a regional language separate from Polish,[[57]](#cite_note-58) while some consider Silesian to be a dialect of Polish.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Many Silesians consider themselves a separate ethnicity and have been advocating for the recognition of Silesian as a [regional language in Poland](/wiki/Languages_of_Poland "Languages of Poland"). The law recognizing it as such was passed by the [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm") and [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Poland "Senate of Poland") in April 2024, but was vetoed by President [Andrzej Duda](/wiki/Andrzej_Duda "Andrzej Duda") in late May 2024.

According to the last official census in Poland in 2011, over half a million people declared Silesian as their native language. Many sociolinguists (e.g. [Tomasz Kamusella](/wiki/Tomasz_Kamusella "Tomasz Kamusella"),[[59]](#cite_note-60) Agnieszka Pianka, Alfred F. Majewicz,[[60]](#cite_note-61) [Tomasz Wicherkiewicz](/wiki/Tomasz_Wicherkiewicz "Tomasz Wicherkiewicz"))[[61]](#cite_note-62) assume that extralinguistic criteria decide whether a lect is an independent language or a dialect: speakers of the [speech variety](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") or/and political decisions, and this is dynamic (i.e. it changes over time). Also, research organizations such as [SIL International](/wiki/SIL_International "SIL International")[[62]](#cite_note-SIL-63) and resources for the academic field of linguistics such as *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")*,[[63]](#cite_note-64) [Linguist List](/wiki/Linguist_List "Linguist List")[[64]](#cite_note-65) and others, for example the [Ministry of Administration and Digitization](/wiki/Ministry_of_Administration_and_Digitization_(Poland) "Ministry of Administration and Digitization (Poland)")[[65]](#cite_note-MoAaD-66) recognized the Silesian language. In July 2007, the Silesian language was recognized by [ISO](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization"), and was attributed an ISO code of szl.

Some additional characteristic but less widespread regional dialects include:

1. The distinctive dialect of the [Gorals](/wiki/Gorals#Language "Gorals") (*Góralski*) occurs in the mountainous area bordering the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"). The Gorals ("Highlanders") take great pride in their culture and the dialect. It exhibits some cultural influences from the [Vlach](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") shepherds in the 14th–17th centuries.[[66]](#cite_note-67)
2. The [Poznański dialect](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gwara_pozna%C5%84ska "pl:Gwara poznańska"), spoken in [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań") and to some extent in the whole region of the former [Prussian Partition](/wiki/Prussian_Partition "Prussian Partition") (excluding [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia")), with noticeable German influences.
3. In the northern and western (formerly German) regions where Poles from the territories annexed by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") resettled after World War II, the older generation speaks a dialect of Polish characteristic of the [Kresy](/wiki/Kresy "Kresy") that includes a longer pronunciation of vowels.
4. Poles living in [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") (particularly in the [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius") region), in Belarus (particularly the northwest), and in the northeast of Poland continue to speak the [Eastern Borderlands](/wiki/Kresy "Kresy") dialect, which sounds "slushed"[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] (in Polish described as *zaciąganie z ruska*, "speaking with a Ruthenian drawl") and is easily distinguishable.
5. Some city dwellers, especially the less affluent population, had their own distinctive dialects – for example, the [Warsaw dialect](/wiki/Warsaw_dialect "Warsaw dialect"), still spoken by some of the population of [Praga](/wiki/Praga "Praga") on the eastern bank of the [Vistula](/wiki/Vistula "Vistula"). However, these city dialects are now[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit) mostly extinct due to assimilation with standard Polish.
6. Many Poles living in emigrant communities (for example, in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")), whose families left Poland just after World War II, retain a number of minor features of Polish vocabulary as spoken in the first half of the 20th century that now sound archaic to contemporary visitors from Poland.

Polish linguistics has been characterized by a strong strive towards promoting [prescriptive](/wiki/Linguistic_prescription "Linguistic prescription") ideas of language intervention and usage uniformity,[[67]](#cite_note-mling-68) along with normatively oriented notions of language "correctness"[[53]](#cite_note-wtad-54) (unusual by Western standards).[[67]](#cite_note-mling-68)

Polish has six oral vowels (seven oral vowels in written form), which are all [monophthongs](/wiki/Monophthong "Monophthong"), and two [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel"). The oral vowels are /[i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel")/ (spelled *i*), /[ɨ](/wiki/Close-mid_central_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid central unrounded vowel")/ (spelled *y* and also transcribed as /ɘ/ or /ɪ/), /[ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel")/ (spelled *e*), /[a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel")/ (spelled *a*), /[ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel")/ (spelled *o*) and /[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/ (spelled *u* and *[ó](/wiki/%C3%93 "Ó")* as separate letters). The nasal vowels are /[ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel")[w̃](/wiki/Nasal_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Nasal labial–velar approximant")/ (spelled *[ę](/wiki/%C4%98 "Ę")*) and /[ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel")[w̃](/wiki/Nasal_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Nasal labial–velar approximant")/ (spelled *[ą](/wiki/%C4%84 "Ą")*). Unlike Czech or Slovak, Polish does not retain phonemic vowel length — the letter *ó*, which formerly represented lengthened /ɔː/ in older forms of the language, is now vestigial and instead corresponds to /u/.

For details, see *[Voicing and devoicing](/wiki/Polish_phonology#Voicing_and_devoicing "Polish phonology")* in the article on Polish phonology.

Most Polish words are [paroxytones](/wiki/Paroxytone "Paroxytone") (that is, the [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") falls on the second-to-last syllable of a polysyllabic word), although there are exceptions).

### Consonant distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Consonant distribution")]

Polish permits complex consonant clusters, which historically often arose from the disappearance of [yers](/wiki/Yer "Yer"). Polish can have word-initial and word-medial clusters of up to four consonants, whereas word-final clusters can have up to five consonants.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Examples of such clusters can be found in words such as [*bezwzględny*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/9b/Pl-bezwzgl%C4%99dny.ogg/Pl-bezwzgl%C4%99dny.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-bezwzgl%C4%99dny.ogg "File:Pl-bezwzględny.ogg") [bɛzˈvzɡlɛndnɨ] ('absolute' or 'heartless', 'ruthless'), [*źdźbło*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/3/38/Pl-%C5%BAd%C5%BAb%C5%82o.ogg/Pl-%C5%BAd%C5%BAb%C5%82o.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-%C5%BAd%C5%BAb%C5%82o.ogg "File:Pl-źdźbło.ogg") [ˈʑd͡ʑbwɔ] ('blade of grass'), [*wstrząs*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/2/26/Pl-wstrz%C4%85s.ogg/Pl-wstrz%C4%85s.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-wstrz%C4%85s.ogg "File:Pl-wstrząs.ogg") [ˈfstʂɔw̃s] ('shock'), and *krnąbrność* [ˈkrnɔmbrnɔɕt͡ɕ] ('disobedience'). A popular Polish [tongue-twister](/wiki/Tongue-twister "Tongue-twister") (from a verse by [Jan Brzechwa](/wiki/Jan_Brzechwa "Jan Brzechwa")) is [*W Szczebrzeszynie chrząszcz brzmi w trzcinie*](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/2/22/Polish_Tongue_twister_-_Chrz%C4%85szcz_-_2.ogg/Polish_Tongue_twister_-_Chrz%C4%85szcz_-_2.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Polish_Tongue_twister_-_Chrz%C4%85szcz_-_2.ogg "File:Polish Tongue twister - Chrząszcz - 2.ogg") [fʂt͡ʂɛbʐɛˈʂɨɲɛ ˈxʂɔw̃ʂt͡ʂ ˈbʐmi fˈtʂt͡ɕiɲɛ] ('In [Szczebrzeszyn](/wiki/Szczebrzeszyn "Szczebrzeszyn") a beetle buzzes in the reed').

Unlike languages such as Czech, Polish does not have [syllabic consonants](/wiki/Syllabic_consonant "Syllabic consonant") – the nucleus of a [syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") is always a vowel.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

The consonant /j/ is restricted to positions adjacent to a vowel. It also cannot precede the letter *y*.

### Prosody

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Prosody")]

The predominant [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") pattern in Polish is penultimate stress – in a word of more than one syllable, the next-to-last syllable is stressed. Alternating preceding syllables carry secondary stress, e.g. in a four-syllable word, where the primary stress is on the third syllable, there will be secondary stress on the first.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Each vowel represents one syllable, although the letter *i* normally does not represent a vowel when it precedes another vowel (it represents /j/, palatalization of the preceding consonant, or both depending on analysis). Also the letters *u* and *i* sometimes represent only semivowels when they follow another vowel, as in *autor* /ˈawtɔr/ ('author'), mostly in loanwords (so not in native *nauka* /naˈu.ka/ 'science, the act of learning', for example, nor in nativized *Mateusz* /maˈte.uʂ/ 'Matthew').

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Styl_urzedowy_-_Polish_sign.jpg/250px-Styl_urzedowy_-_Polish_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Styl_urzedowy_-_Polish_sign.jpg)

A formal-tone informative sign in Polish, with a composition of vowels and consonants and a mixture of long, medium and short [syllables](/wiki/Syllables "Syllables")

Some [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword"), particularly from the [classical languages](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language"), have the stress on the antepenultimate (third-from-last) syllable. For example, *fizyka* (/ˈfizɨka/) ('physics') is stressed on the first syllable. This may lead to a rare phenomenon of minimal pairs differing only in stress placement, for example *muzyka* /ˈmuzɨka/ 'music' vs. *muzyka* /muˈzɨka/ – genitive singular of *muzyk* 'musician'. When additional syllables are added to such words through [inflection](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") or [suffixation](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix"), the stress normally becomes regular. For example, *uniwersytet* (/uɲiˈvɛrsɨtɛt/, 'university') has irregular stress on the third (or antepenultimate) syllable, but the genitive *uniwersytetu* (/uɲivɛrsɨˈtɛtu/) and derived adjective *uniwersytecki* (/uɲivɛrsɨˈtɛt͡skʲi/) have regular stress on the penultimate syllables. Loanwords generally become nativized to have penultimate stress.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGussmann20079-72) In psycholinguistic experiments, speakers of Polish have been demonstrated to be sensitive to the distinction between regular penultimate and exceptional antepenultimate stress.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

Another class of exceptions is verbs with the conditional endings *-by, -bym, -byśmy*, etc. These endings are not counted in determining the position of the stress; for example, ***zro**biłbym* ('I would do') is stressed on the first syllable, and *zro**bi**libyśmy* ('we would do') on the second. According to [prescriptive authorities](/wiki/Linguistic_prescription "Linguistic prescription"), the same applies to the first and second person plural past tense endings *-śmy, -ście*, although this rule is often ignored in [colloquial speech](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") (so *zro**bi**liśmy* 'we did' should be prescriptively stressed on the second syllable, although in [practice](/wiki/Usus "Usus") it is commonly stressed on the third as *zrobi**li**śmy*).[[73]](#cite_note-74) These irregular stress patterns are explained by the fact that these endings are detachable [clitics](/wiki/Clitic "Clitic") rather than true verbal inflections: for example, instead of ***ko**go zoba**czy**liście?* ('whom did you see?') it is possible to say ***ko**goście zoba**czy**li?* – here *kogo* retains its usual stress (first syllable) in spite of the attachment of the clitic. Reanalysis of the endings as inflections when attached to verbs causes the different colloquial stress patterns. These stress patterns are considered part of a "usable" norm of standard Polish - in contrast to the "model" ("high") norm.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

Some common word combinations are stressed as if they were a single word. This applies in particular to many combinations of preposition plus a personal pronoun, such as ***do** niej* ('to her'), ***na** nas* ('on us'), *prze**ze** mnie* ('because of me'), all stressed on the bolded syllable.

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Orthography")]

Main articles: [Polish orthography](/wiki/Polish_orthography "Polish orthography") and [Polish Braille](/wiki/Polish_Braille "Polish Braille")

See also: [History of Polish orthography](/wiki/History_of_Polish_orthography "History of Polish orthography")

The [Polish alphabet](/wiki/Polish_alphabet "Polish alphabet") derives from the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") but includes certain additional letters formed using [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic"). The Polish alphabet was one of three major forms of Latin-based orthography developed for Western and some South Slavic languages, the others being [Czech orthography](/wiki/Czech_orthography "Czech orthography") and [Croatian orthography](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), the last of these being a 19th-century invention trying to make a compromise between the first two. [Kashubian](/wiki/Kashubian_language "Kashubian language") uses a Polish-based system, [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language") uses a Czech-based system, and [Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") follows the Croatian one; the [Sorbian languages](/wiki/Sorbian_languages "Sorbian languages") blend the Polish and the Czech ones.

Historically, Poland's once diverse and multi-ethnic population utilized many forms of scripture to write Polish. For instance, [Lipka Tatars](/wiki/Lipka_Tatars "Lipka Tatars") and [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") inhabiting the eastern parts of the former [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") wrote Polish in the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet") alphabet.[[75]](#cite_note-76) The [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") is used to a certain extent today by [Polish speakers](/wiki/Poles_in_Belarus "Poles in Belarus") in [Western Belarus](/wiki/Western_Belarus "Western Belarus"), especially for religious texts.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

The diacritics used in the Polish alphabet are the *kreska* (graphically similar to the [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent")) over the letters *ć, ń, ó, ś, ź* and through the letter in *ł*; the *kropka* (superior dot) over the letter *ż*, and the *[ogonek](/wiki/Ogonek "Ogonek")* ("little tail") under the letters *ą, ę*. The letters *q, v, x* are used only in foreign words and names.[[17]](#cite_note-sjp.pwn.pl-18)

Polish orthography is largely [phonemic](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme")—there is a consistent correspondence between letters (or [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") and [trigraphs](/wiki/Trigraph_(orthography) "Trigraph (orthography)")) and phonemes (for exceptions see below). Voiced consonant letters frequently come to represent voiceless sounds (as shown in the tables); this occurs at the end of words and in certain clusters, due to the neutralization mentioned in the *[Phonology](#Phonology)* section above. Occasionally also voiceless consonant letters can represent voiced sounds in clusters.

The spelling rule for the palatal sounds /[ɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiceless alveolo-palatal fricative")/, /[ʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiced alveolo-palatal fricative")/, /[tɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate")/, /[dʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiced alveolo-palatal affricate")/ and /[ɲ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_nasal "Voiced alveolo-palatal nasal")/ is as follows: before the vowel *i* the plain letters *s, z, c, dz, n* are used; before other vowels the combinations *si, zi, ci, dzi, ni* are used; when not followed by a vowel the diacritic forms *ś, ź, ć, dź, ń* are used. For example, the *s* in *siwy* ("grey-haired"), the *si* in *siarka* ("sulfur") and the *ś* in *święty* ("holy") all represent the sound /[ɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiceless alveolo-palatal fricative")/. The exceptions to the above rule are certain loanwords from Latin, Italian, French, Russian or English—where *s* before *i* is pronounced as *s*, e.g. *sinus*, *sinologia*, *do re mi fa sol la si do*, *Saint-Simon i saint-simoniści*, *Sierioża*, *Siergiej*, *Singapur*, *singiel*. In other loanwords the vowel *i* is changed to *y*, e.g. *asymilacja*.

The following table shows the correspondence between the sounds and spelling:

[Digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") and [trigraphs](/wiki/Trigraph_(orthography) "Trigraph (orthography)") are used:

| Phonemic value | Single letter/Digraph (in pausa or  before a consonant) | Digraph/Trigraph (before a vowel) | Single letter/Digraph (before the vowel *i*) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| /[tɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate")/ | *ć* | *ci* | *c* |
| /[dʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiced alveolo-palatal affricate")/ | *dź* | *dzi* | *dz* |
| /[ɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiceless alveolo-palatal fricative")/ | *ś* | *si* | *s* |
| /[ʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiced alveolo-palatal fricative")/ | *ź* | *zi* | *z* |
| /[ɲ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_nasal "Voiced alveolo-palatal nasal")/ | *ń* | *ni* | *n* |

Similar principles apply to /[kʲ](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)")/, /[ɡʲ](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)")/, /[xʲ](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)")/ and [/lʲ/](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)"), except that these can only occur before vowels, so the spellings are *k, g, (c)h, l* before *i*, and *ki, gi, (c)hi, li* otherwise. Most Polish speakers, however, do not consider palatalization of *k, g, (c)h* or *l* as creating new sounds.

Except in the cases mentioned above, the letter *i* if followed by another vowel in the same word usually represents /[j](/wiki/Voiced_palatal_approximant "Voiced palatal approximant")/, yet a palatalization of the previous consonant is always assumed.

The reverse case, where the consonant remains unpalatalized but is followed by a palatalized consonant, is written by using *j* instead of *i*: for example, *zjeść*, "to eat up".

The letters *ą* and *ę*, when followed by plosives and affricates, represent an oral vowel followed by a nasal consonant, rather than a nasal vowel. For example, *ą* in *dąb* ("oak") is pronounced [[ɔm]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish"), and *ę* in *tęcza* ("rainbow") is pronounced [[ɛn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") (the nasal assimilates to the following consonant). When followed by *l* or *ł* (for example *przyjęli*, *przyjęły*), *ę* is pronounced as just *e*. When *ę* is at the end of the word it is often pronounced as just [[ɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish").

Depending on the word, the phoneme /[x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative")/ can be spelt *h* or *ch*, the phoneme /[ʐ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_fricative "Voiced retroflex fricative")/ can be spelt *ż* or *rz*, and /[u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")/ can be spelt *u* or *ó*. In several cases it determines the meaning, for example: *może* ("maybe") and *morze* ("sea").

In occasional words, letters that normally form a digraph are pronounced separately. For example, *rz* represents /rz/, not /[ʐ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_fricative "Voiced retroflex fricative")/, in words like *zamarzać* ("freeze") and in the name **[Tarzan](/wiki/Tarzan "Tarzan")**.

Doubled letters are usually pronounced as a single, [lengthened](/wiki/Gemination#Polish "Gemination") consonant, however, some speakers might pronounce the combination as two separate sounds.

There are certain clusters where a written consonant would not be pronounced. For example, the *ł* in the word *jabłko* ("apple") might be omitted in ordinary speech, leading to the pronunciation *japko*.

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Polish grammar](/wiki/Polish_grammar "Polish grammar")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Polish is a highly [fusional language](/wiki/Fusional_language "Fusional language") with relatively free [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order"), although the dominant arrangement is [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") (SVO). There are no [articles](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)"), and subject pronouns are often [dropped](/wiki/Pro-drop_language "Pro-drop language").

[Nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun") belong to one of three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"): masculine, feminine and neuter. The masculine gender is also divided into subgenders: animate vs inanimate in the singular, human vs nonhuman in the plural. There are seven [cases](/wiki/Case_(grammar) "Case (grammar)"): nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, instrumental, locative and vocative.

[Adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") agree with nouns in terms of gender, case, and number. [Attributive adjectives](/wiki/Attributive_adjective "Attributive adjective") most commonly precede the noun, although in certain cases, especially in fixed phrases (like *język polski*, "Polish (language)"), the noun may come first; the rule of thumb is that generic descriptive adjectives normally precede (e.g. *piękny kwiat*, "beautiful flower") while categorizing adjectives often follow the noun (e.g. *węgiel kamienny*, "black coal"). Most short adjectives and their derived [adverbs](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") form [comparatives](/wiki/Comparative "Comparative") and [superlatives](/wiki/Superlative "Superlative") by inflection (the superlative is formed by prefixing *naj-* to the comparative).

[Verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb") are of imperfective or perfective [aspect](/wiki/Aspect_(grammar) "Aspect (grammar)"), often occurring in pairs. Imperfective verbs have a present tense, past tense, compound future tense (except for *być* "to be", which has a simple future *będę* etc., this in turn being used to form the compound future of other verbs), subjunctive/conditional (formed with the detachable particle *by*), imperatives, an infinitive, present participle, present gerund and past participle. Perfective verbs have a simple future tense (formed like the present tense of imperfective verbs), past tense, subjunctive/conditional, imperatives, infinitive, present gerund and past participle. Conjugated verb forms agree with their subject in terms of person, number, and (in the case of past tense and subjunctive/conditional forms) gender.

[Passive](/wiki/Passive_voice "Passive voice")-type constructions can be made using the auxiliary *być* or *zostać* ("become") with the passive participle. There is also an impersonal construction where the active verb is used (in third person singular) with no subject, but with the reflexive pronoun *się* present to indicate a general, unspecified subject (as in *pije się wódkę* "vodka is being drunk"—note that *wódka* appears in the accusative). A similar sentence type in the past tense uses the passive participle with the ending *-o*, as in *widziano ludzi* ("people were seen"). As in other Slavic languages, there are also subjectless sentences formed using such words as *można* ("it is possible") together with an infinitive.

[Yes–no questions](/wiki/Yes%E2%80%93no_question "Yes–no question") (both direct and indirect) are formed by placing the word *czy* ("whether") at the start, although it's often omitted in casual speech. Negation uses the word *nie*, before the verb or other item being negated; *nie* is still added before the verb even if the sentence also contains other negatives such as *nigdy* ("never") or *nic* ("nothing"), effectively creating a [double negative](/wiki/Double_negative "Double negative").

[Cardinal numbers](/wiki/Cardinal_number_(linguistics) "Cardinal number (linguistics)") have a complex system of inflection and agreement. Zero and cardinal numbers higher than five (except for those ending with the digit 2, 3 or 4 but not ending with 12, 13 or 14) govern the [genitive case](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") rather than the nominative or accusative. Special forms of numbers ([collective numerals](/wiki/Collective_numeral "Collective numeral")) are used with certain classes of noun, which include *dziecko* ("child") and [exclusively plural](/wiki/Plurale_tantum "Plurale tantum") nouns such as *drzwi* ("door").

Poland was once a multi-ethnic nation with many minorities that contributed to the Polish language.

1. Top left: [cauliflower](/wiki/Cauliflower "Cauliflower") (Polish *kalafior* from [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") *cavolfiore*).
2. Top right: [rope](/wiki/Rope "Rope") (*sznur* from [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") *Schnur*).
3. Bottom left: [shark](/wiki/Shark "Shark") (*rekin* from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") *requin*).
4. Bottom right: [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname") (*ksywa* (colloquial) from [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language") כּתיבֿה *ksive*)

Polish has, over the centuries, borrowed a number of words from other languages. When borrowing, pronunciation was adapted to Polish phonemes and spelling was altered to match [Polish orthography](/wiki/Polish_orthography "Polish orthography"). In addition, word endings are liberally applied to almost any word to produce [verbs](/wiki/Verb "Verb"), [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), [adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), as well as adding the appropriate endings for cases of nouns, adjectives, [diminutives](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive"), double-diminutives, [augmentatives](/wiki/Augmentative "Augmentative"), etc.

Depending on the historical period, borrowing has proceeded from various languages. Notable influences have been [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") (10th–18th centuries),[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language") (10th and 14th–15th centuries), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (16th–17th centuries),[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (17th–19th centuries),[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) German (13–15th and 18th–20th centuries), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") (15th–16th centuries)[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language") (17th century). Currently, English words are the most common imports to Polish.[[78]](#cite_note-Knara-2017-79)

Loanwords make up 26.2% of the Polish vocabulary, with 36.3% originating from Latin, 19.7% from German, 15.9% from French, 7.1% from Czech, 3.8% from Greek, 3.5% from English, 3.1% from Italian, 1.9% from Ukrainian and Belarusian, 1.4% from Russian, and 1.5% from other languages; 5.9% are artificial borrowings (usually [compound words](/wiki/Compound_words "Compound words") of undetermined origin).[[79]](#cite_note-80)

The Latin language, for a very long time the only official language of the Polish state, has had a great influence on Polish. Many Polish words were direct borrowings or calques (e.g. *[rzeczpospolita](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rzeczpospolita "wikt:rzeczpospolita")* from *res publica*) from Latin. [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") was known to a larger or smaller degree by most of the numerous [szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta") in the 16th to 18th centuries (and it continued to be extensively taught at secondary schools until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")). Apart from dozens of loanwords, its influence can also be seen in a number of verbatim Latin phrases in [Polish literature](/wiki/Polish_literature "Polish literature") (especially from the 19th century and earlier).
During the 12th and 13th centuries, Mongolian words were brought to the Polish language during wars with the armies of [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan") and his descendants, e.g. *dzida* (spear) and *szereg* (a line or row).[[78]](#cite_note-Knara-2017-79)

Words from [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), an important influence during the 10th and 14th–15th centuries include *[sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm")*, *hańba* and *brama*.[[78]](#cite_note-Knara-2017-79)

In 1518, the Polish king [Sigismund I the Old](/wiki/Sigismund_I_of_Poland "Sigismund I of Poland") married [Bona Sforza](/wiki/Bona_Sforza "Bona Sforza"), the niece of the Holy Roman emperor Maximilian, who introduced Italian cuisine to Poland, especially vegetables.[[80]](#cite_note-ciekawostkihistoryczne.pl-81) Hence, words from Italian include *pomidor* from "pomodoro" ([tomato](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato")), *kalafior* from "cavolfiore" ([cauliflower](/wiki/Cauliflower "Cauliflower")), and *pomarańcza*, a portmanteau from Italian "pomo" ([pome](/wiki/Pome "Pome")) plus "arancio" (orange). A later word of Italian origin is *autostrada* (from Italian "autostrada", highway).[[80]](#cite_note-ciekawostkihistoryczne.pl-81)

In the 18th century, with the rising prominence of France in Europe, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") supplanted Latin as an important source of words. Some French borrowings also date from the Napoleonic era, when the Poles were enthusiastic supporters of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"). Examples include *ekran* (from French "écran", screen), *abażur* ("abat-jour", lamp shade), *biuro* ("bureau", [office](/wiki/Office "Office")), *biżuteria* ("bijou", [jewelry](/wiki/Jewellery "Jewellery")), *rekin* ("requin", [shark](/wiki/Shark "Shark")), *meble* ("meuble", furniture), *bagaż* ("bagage", luggage), *walizka* ("valise", suitcase), *fotel* ("fauteuil", armchair), *plaża* ("plage", beach) and *koszmar* ("cauchemar", [nightmare](/wiki/Nightmare "Nightmare")). Some place names have also been adapted from French, such as the [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") boroughs of [Żoliborz](/wiki/%C5%BBoliborz "Żoliborz") ("joli bord" = beautiful riverside), [Marymont](/wiki/Marymont "Marymont") ("Marie mont" = Mary's hill) as well as the town of [Żyrardów](/wiki/%C5%BByrard%C3%B3w "Żyrardów") (from the name [Girard](/wiki/Philippe_de_Girard "Philippe de Girard"), with the Polish suffix -ów attached to refer to the founder of the town).[[81]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Pink_Birkin_bag.jpg/250px-Pink_Birkin_bag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pink_Birkin_bag.jpg)

Common [handbag](/wiki/Handbag "Handbag") in Polish is called a *torba*, a word directly derived from the [Turkish language](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"). Turkish loanwords are common as Poland bordered the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") for centuries.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Many words were borrowed from the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language") from the sizable German population in Polish cities during medieval times. German words found in the Polish language are often connected with trade, the building industry, civic rights and city life. Some words were assimilated verbatim, for example *handel* (trade) and *dach* (roof); others are pronounced similarly, but differ in writing *Schnur*—*sznur* (cord). As a result of being neighbors with Germany, Polish has many German expressions which have become literally translated ([calques](/wiki/Calques "Calques")). The regional [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") of [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia") and [Masuria](/wiki/Masuria "Masuria") (Modern Polish [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia")) have noticeably more German loanwords than other varieties.

The [contacts with Ottoman Turkey](/wiki/Poland-Turkey_relations "Poland-Turkey relations") in the 17th century brought many new words, some of them still in use, such as: *jar* ("yar" deep valley), *szaszłyk* ("şişlik" shish kebab), *filiżanka* ("fincan" cup), *arbuz* ("karpuz" [watermelon](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon")), *dywan* ("divan" carpet),[[82]](#cite_note-83) etc.

From the founding of the Kingdom of Poland in 1025 through the early years of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") created in 1569, Poland was the most tolerant country of Jews in Europe. Known as the "[paradise for the Jews](/wiki/Heaven_for_the_nobles,_Purgatory_for_the_townspeople,_Hell_for_the_peasants,_and_Paradise_for_the_Jews "Heaven for the nobles, Purgatory for the townspeople, Hell for the peasants, and Paradise for the Jews")",[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-85) it became a shelter for persecuted and expelled European Jewish communities and the home to the world's largest Jewish community of the time. As a result, many Polish words come from [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), spoken by the large [Polish Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland "History of the Jews in Poland") population that existed until the [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"). Borrowed Yiddish words include *bachor* (an unruly boy or child), *bajzel* (slang for mess), *belfer* (slang for teacher), *ciuchy* (slang for clothing), *cymes* (slang for very tasty food), *geszeft* (slang for business), *kitel* (slang for apron), *machlojka* (slang for scam), *mamona* (money), *manele* (slang for oddments), *myszygene* (slang for lunatic), *pinda* (slang for girl, pejoratively), *plajta* (slang for bankruptcy), *rejwach* (noise), *szmal* (slang for money), and *trefny* (dodgy).[[85]](#cite_note-86)

The mountain dialects of the [Górale](/wiki/Gorals "Gorals") in southern Poland, have quite a number of words borrowed from [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") (e.g. *baca*, *gazda*, *juhas*, *hejnał*) and [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") as a result of historical contacts with Hungarian-dominated [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") and Wallachian herders who travelled north along the [Carpathians](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains").[[86]](#cite_note-87)

[Thieves' slang](/wiki/Grypsera "Grypsera") includes such words as *kimać* (to sleep) or *majcher* (knife) of Greek origin, considered then unknown to the outside world.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

In addition, Turkish and Tatar have exerted influence upon the vocabulary of war, names of oriental costumes etc.[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) Russian borrowings began to make their way into Polish from the second half of the 19th century on.[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78)

Polish has also received an intensive number of English loanwords, particularly after World War II.[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78) Recent loanwords come primarily from the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), mainly those that have [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") or [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") roots, for example *komputer* (computer), *korupcja* (from 'corruption', but sense restricted to 'bribery') etc. Concatenation of parts of words (e.g. *auto-moto*), which is not native to Polish but common in English, for example, is also sometimes used. When borrowing English words, Polish often changes their spelling. For example, Latin suffix '-tio' corresponds to *-cja*. To make the word plural, *-cja* becomes *-cje*. Examples of this include *inauguracja* (inauguration), *dewastacja* (devastation), *recepcja* (reception), *konurbacja* (conurbation) and *konotacje* (connotations). Also, the digraph *qu* becomes *kw* (*kwadrant* = quadrant; *kworum* = quorum).

## Loanwords from Polish in other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Loanwords from Polish in other languages")]

See also: [List of English words of Polish origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Polish_origin "List of English words of Polish origin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Fishing_-_The_Noun_Project.svg/250px-Fishing_-_The_Noun_Project.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Fishing_-_The_Noun_Project.svg)

There are numerous words in both Polish and [Yiddish (Jewish)](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language") languages which are near-identical due to the large Jewish minority that once inhabited Poland. One example is the [fishing rod](/wiki/Fishing_rod "Fishing rod"), ווענטקע (*ventke*), borrowed directly from Polish *wędka*.

The Polish language has influenced others. Particular influences appear in other Slavic languages and in [German](/wiki/History_of_the_Germans_in_Poland "History of the Germans in Poland") — due to their proximity and shared borders.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Examples of loanwords include German *Grenze* (border),[[89]](#cite_note-90) [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") and [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans") *grens* from Polish *granica*; German *Peitzker* from Polish *piskorz* (weatherfish); German *Zobel*, French *zibeline*, [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") *sobel*, and English *sable* from Polish *soból*; and *[ogonek](/wiki/Ogonek "Ogonek")* ("little tail") — the word describing a diacritic hook-sign added below some letters in various alphabets. The common [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") word *[quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz")* comes from the dialectical [Old Polish](/wiki/Old_Polish "Old Polish") *kwardy*. "[Szmata](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Szmata "pl:Szmata")," a Polish, Slovak and [Ruthenian](/wiki/Rusyn_language "Rusyn language") word for "mop" or "rag", became part of [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"). The Polish language exerted significant lexical influence upon [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"), particularly in the fields of abstract and technical terminology; for example, the Ukrainian word панство *panstvo* (country) is derived from Polish *państwo*.[[90]](#cite_note-Timothy_Shopen-1987-91) The Polish influence on Ukrainian is particularly marked on western Ukrainian dialects in western Ukraine, which for centuries was under Polish cultural domination.[[90]](#cite_note-Timothy_Shopen-1987-91)[[23]](#cite_note-Swan-2002-24)[[77]](#cite_note-Istvan_Fodor,_Claude_Hagege-1983-78)[[91]](#cite_note-92)

There are a substantial number of Polish words which officially became part of Yiddish, once the main language of European [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"). These include basic items, objects or terms such as a [bread bun](/wiki/Bun "Bun") (Polish *bułka*, Yiddish בולקע *bulke*), a [fishing rod](/wiki/Fishing_rod "Fishing rod") (*wędka*, ווענטקע *ventke*), an [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") (*dąb*, דעמב *demb*), a [meadow](/wiki/Meadow "Meadow") (*łąka*, לאָנקע *lonke*), a [moustache](/wiki/Moustache "Moustache") (*wąsy*, וואָנצעס *vontses*) and a [bladder](/wiki/Bladder "Bladder") (*pęcherz*, פּענכער *penkher*).[[92]](#cite_note-93)

Quite a few culinary loanwords exist in German and in other languages, some of which describe distinctive features of Polish cuisine. These include German and English *[Quark](/wiki/Quark_(dairy_product) "Quark (dairy product)")* from *twaróg* (a kind of fresh cheese) and German *Gurke*, English *gherkin* from *ogórek* (cucumber). The word *[pierogi](/wiki/Pierogi "Pierogi")* (Polish dumplings) has spread internationally, as well as *[pączki](/wiki/P%C4%85czki "Pączki")* (Polish donuts)[[93]](#cite_note-94) and kiełbasa (sausage, e.g. *kolbaso* in [Esperanto](/wiki/Esperanto "Esperanto")). As far as *pierogi* concerned, the original Polish word is already in plural (sing. *pieróg*, plural *pierogi*; stem *pierog-*, plural ending *-i*; NB. *o* becomes *ó* in a closed syllable, like here in singular), yet it is commonly used with the English plural ending *-s* in Canada and United States of America, *pierogis*, thus making it a "double plural". A similar situation happened with the Polish loanword from English *czipsy* ("potato chips")—from English *chips* being already plural in the original (*chip* + *-s*), yet it has obtained the Polish plural ending *-y*.[[b]](#cite_note-chip-95)

It is believed that the English word *[spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce")* was derived from *Prusy*, the Polish name for the region of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"). It became *spruce* because in Polish, *z Prus*, sounded like "spruce" in English (transl. "from [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")") and was a generic term for commodities brought to England by [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") merchants and because the tree was believed to have come from Polish Ducal Prussia.[[94]](#cite_note-etymonline-96) However, it can be argued that the word is actually derived from the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") term *Pruce*, meaning literally Prussia.[[94]](#cite_note-etymonline-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Manuscript_of_Pan_Tadeusz_8pl.jpg/250px-Manuscript_of_Pan_Tadeusz_8pl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manuscript_of_Pan_Tadeusz_8pl.jpg)

The manuscript of *[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* held at [Ossolineum](/wiki/Ossolineum "Ossolineum") in [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"). Adam Mickiewicz's signature is visible.

The Polish language started to be used in literature in the Late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").[[95]](#cite_note-97) Notable works include the *[Holy Cross Sermons](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Sermons "Holy Cross Sermons")* (13th/14th century), *[Bogurodzica](/wiki/Bogurodzica "Bogurodzica")* (15th century) and *[Master Polikarp's Dialog with Death](/wiki/Master_Polikarp%27s_Dialog_with_Death "Master Polikarp's Dialog with Death")* (15th century). The most influential Renaissance-era literary figures in Poland were poet [Jan Kochanowski](/wiki/Jan_Kochanowski "Jan Kochanowski") (*[Laments](/wiki/Laments_(Kochanowski) "Laments (Kochanowski)")*), [Mikołaj Rej](/wiki/Miko%C5%82aj_Rej "Mikołaj Rej") and [Piotr Skarga](/wiki/Piotr_Skarga "Piotr Skarga") (*[The Lives of the Saints](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Saints_(Skarga) "Lives of the Saints (Skarga)")*) who established poetic patterns that would become integral to the Polish literary language and laid foundations for the modern Polish grammar.[[96]](#cite_note-98) During the [Age of Enlightenment in Poland](/wiki/Enlightenment_in_Poland "Enlightenment in Poland"), [Ignacy Krasicki](/wiki/Ignacy_Krasicki "Ignacy Krasicki"), known as "the Prince of Poets", wrote the first Polish novel called *[The Adventures of Mr. Nicholas Wisdom](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Mr._Nicholas_Wisdom "The Adventures of Mr. Nicholas Wisdom")* as well as *[Fables and Parables](/wiki/Fables_and_Parables "Fables and Parables")*.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Another significant work form this period is *[The Manuscript Found in Saragossa](/wiki/The_Manuscript_Found_in_Saragossa "The Manuscript Found in Saragossa")* by [Jan Potocki](/wiki/Jan_Potocki "Jan Potocki").

In the [Romantic Era](/wiki/Romanticism_in_Poland "Romanticism in Poland"), the most celebrated national poets, referred to as the [Three Bards](/wiki/Three_Bards "Three Bards"), were [Adam Mickiewicz](/wiki/Adam_Mickiewicz "Adam Mickiewicz") (*[Pan Tadeusz](/wiki/Pan_Tadeusz "Pan Tadeusz")* and *[Dziady](/wiki/Dziady_(poem) "Dziady (poem)")*), [Juliusz Słowacki](/wiki/Juliusz_S%C5%82owacki "Juliusz Słowacki") (*[Balladyna](/wiki/Balladyna_(drama) "Balladyna (drama)")*) and [Zygmunt Krasiński](/wiki/Zygmunt_Krasi%C5%84ski "Zygmunt Krasiński") (*[The Undivine Comedy](/wiki/The_Undivine_Comedy "The Undivine Comedy")*). Poet and dramatist [Cyprian Norwid](/wiki/Cyprian_Norwid "Cyprian Norwid") is regarded by some scholars as the "Fourth Bard". Important positivist writers include [Bolesław Prus](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_Prus "Bolesław Prus") (*[The Doll](/wiki/The_Doll_(Prus_novel) "The Doll (Prus novel)")*, *[Pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh_(Prus_novel) "Pharaoh (Prus novel)")*), [Henryk Sienkiewicz](/wiki/Henryk_Sienkiewicz "Henryk Sienkiewicz") (author of numerous historical novels the most internationally acclaimed of which is *[Quo Vadis](/wiki/Quo_Vadis_(novel) "Quo Vadis (novel)")*), [Maria Konopnicka](/wiki/Maria_Konopnicka "Maria Konopnicka") (*[Rota](/wiki/Rota_(poem) "Rota (poem)")*), [Eliza Orzeszkowa](/wiki/Eliza_Orzeszkowa "Eliza Orzeszkowa") (*[Nad Niemnem](/wiki/Nad_Niemnem "Nad Niemnem")*), [Adam Asnyk](/wiki/Adam_Asnyk "Adam Asnyk") and [Gabriela Zapolska](/wiki/Gabriela_Zapolska "Gabriela Zapolska") (*[The Morality of Mrs. Dulska](/wiki/The_Morality_of_Mrs._Dulska "The Morality of Mrs. Dulska")*). The period known as [Young Poland](/wiki/Young_Poland "Young Poland") produced such renowned literary figures as [Stanisław Wyspiański](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Wyspia%C5%84ski "Stanisław Wyspiański") (*[The Wedding](/wiki/The_Wedding_(1901_play) "The Wedding (1901 play)")*), [Stefan Żeromski](/wiki/Stefan_%C5%BBeromski "Stefan Żeromski") (*[Homeless People](/wiki/Ludzie_bezdomni "Ludzie bezdomni")*, *[The Spring to Come](/wiki/The_Spring_to_Come "The Spring to Come")*), [Władysław Reymont](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Reymont "Władysław Reymont") (*[The Peasants](/wiki/The_Peasants "The Peasants")*) and [Leopold Staff](/wiki/Leopold_Staff "Leopold Staff"). The prominent interbellum period authors include [Maria Dąbrowska](/wiki/Maria_D%C4%85browska "Maria Dąbrowska") (*[Nights and Days](/wiki/Nights_and_Days "Nights and Days")*), [Stanisław Ignacy Witkiewicz](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Ignacy_Witkiewicz "Stanisław Ignacy Witkiewicz") (*[Insatiability](/wiki/Insatiability "Insatiability")*), [Julian Tuwim](/wiki/Julian_Tuwim "Julian Tuwim"), [Bruno Schulz](/wiki/Bruno_Schulz "Bruno Schulz"), [Bolesław Leśmian](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_Le%C5%9Bmian "Bolesław Leśmian"), [Witold Gombrowicz](/wiki/Witold_Gombrowicz "Witold Gombrowicz") and [Zuzanna Ginczanka](/wiki/Zuzanna_Ginczanka "Zuzanna Ginczanka").

Other notable writers and poets from Poland active during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and after are [Aleksander Kamiński](/wiki/Aleksander_Kami%C5%84ski "Aleksander Kamiński"), [Zbigniew Herbert](/wiki/Zbigniew_Herbert "Zbigniew Herbert"), [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem"), [Zofia Nałkowska](/wiki/Zofia_Na%C5%82kowska "Zofia Nałkowska"), [Tadeusz Borowski](/wiki/Tadeusz_Borowski "Tadeusz Borowski"), [Sławomir Mrożek](/wiki/S%C5%82awomir_Mro%C5%BCek "Sławomir Mrożek"), [Krzysztof Kamil Baczyński](/wiki/Krzysztof_Kamil_Baczy%C5%84ski "Krzysztof Kamil Baczyński"), [Julia Hartwig](/wiki/Julia_Hartwig "Julia Hartwig"), [Marek Krajewski](/wiki/Marek_Krajewski "Marek Krajewski"), [Joanna Bator](/wiki/Joanna_Bator "Joanna Bator"), [Andrzej Sapkowski](/wiki/Andrzej_Sapkowski "Andrzej Sapkowski"), [Adam Zagajewski](/wiki/Adam_Zagajewski "Adam Zagajewski"), [Dorota Masłowska](/wiki/Dorota_Mas%C5%82owska "Dorota Masłowska"), [Jerzy Pilch](/wiki/Jerzy_Pilch "Jerzy Pilch"), [Ryszard Kapuściński](/wiki/Ryszard_Kapu%C5%9Bci%C5%84ski "Ryszard Kapuściński") and [Andrzej Stasiuk](/wiki/Andrzej_Stasiuk "Andrzej Stasiuk").

Five people writing in the Polish language have been awarded the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_in_Literature "List of Nobel laureates in Literature"): [Henryk Sienkiewicz](/wiki/Henryk_Sienkiewicz "Henryk Sienkiewicz") (1905), [Władysław Reymont](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Reymont "Władysław Reymont") (1924), [Czesław Miłosz](/wiki/Czes%C5%82aw_Mi%C5%82osz "Czesław Miłosz") (1980), [Wisława Szymborska](/wiki/Wis%C5%82awa_Szymborska "Wisława Szymborska") (1996) and [Olga Tokarczuk](/wiki/Olga_Tokarczuk "Olga Tokarczuk") (2018).

## Sample text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Polish_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sample text")]

Polish pronunciation

Article 1 of the *[Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights")* in Polish:[[98]](#cite_note-100)

:   *Wszyscy ludzie rodzą się wolni i równi pod względem swej godności i swych praw. Są oni obdarzeni rozumem i sumieniem i powinni postępować wobec innych w duchu braterstwa.*

Article 1 of the *Universal Declaration of Human Rights* in English:[[99]](#cite_note-101)

:   *All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.*
